basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Māʿūn — “Small Kindnesses” — is an early Meccan sūra, named after its last word: māʿūn (مَاعُون), a wide term naming at once the utensils people lend each other (the pickax, the pot, the bucket), the alms that are due, and more broadly every small service by which common life is held together. Seven brief verses set out a strong thesis: to deny religion (dīn) is not first a wrong opinion — it is a conduct. The one who does not feed, who drives away the orphan, who prays without heart, who acts to be seen — that one, in the Quran, treats religion as a lie. The sūra thus opens an essential equivalence: true devotion and care for the weakest cannot be separated.

versets.trad: Have you seen the one who treats religion as a lie?
versets.commentaire: yukadhdhibu bi-d-dīn (يُكَذِّبُ بِالدِّين): “to treat religion as a lie”. The verb kadhdhaba (كَذَّبَ) — intensive form of the root k-dh-b, “to lie” — means “to give the lie to”, “to hold as a liar”. al-Dīn (الدِّين) can be the revealed religion, but also “the judgment” (the Day of dīn). Commentators note that this first verse governs all that follows: if denying judgment is the root of every sin, the verse names not an opinion about God but an attitude that will show itself in the deeds described next.

mots.def: prefixed interrogative particle: is it that…?; introduces a question.
raʾā: to see; perfect, 2nd pers. masc. sing.: have you seen.
masc. sing. relative pronoun: the one who.
kadhdhaba: intensive form of kadhaba (to lie); “to hold as a liar”, “to give the lie to”.
dīn: religion, way, due order; also: judgment (the Day of dīn). Preposition bi- + noun in the genitive.

versets.trad: That is the one who drives away the orphan,
versets.commentaire: yaduʿʿu (يَدُعُّ): to push away roughly, to repulse, to brush off. The verb is harsh. And its object is the most vulnerable: al-yatīm (اليَتِيم), the orphan of the father. The sūra does not say “the one who does not love the orphan”; it says: the one who pushes him away with the back of his hand. The Quran elsewhere names also “devouring the inheritance of orphans” among the grave faults (4:2–3). True religion begins there — not in the abstract, but in the face of one who has no one.

mots.def: linking particle: then, so.
masc. sing. far demonstrative: that one.
masc. sing. relative pronoun.
daʿʿa: to push roughly, to repulse with a stroke; a harsh verb naming a curt rejection.
yatīm: orphan (of the father, in Arab culture; figure of absolute vulnerability).

versets.trad: and does not urge the feeding of the indigent.
versets.commentaire: yaḥuḍḍu (يَحُضُّ): to urge, to incite to. The verse does not only say that this man does not feed the indigent — it says he does not urge others to do so. The fault here is twofold: not to give, and not to encourage giving. Another reading hears: “he does not urge the giving of the food due to the indigent”, for the Quran elsewhere recalls that in the wealth of the believer “there is an acknowledged right for the beggar and the deprived” (70:24–25). Food is not a favour but a due.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
negative particle.
ḥaḍḍa: to urge, to incite, to exhort to do something; with lā: he does not urge.
preposition: upon, regarding; here introduces the object of urging.
ṭaʿām: food, what is eaten.
miskīn: the indigent, the destitute; from the root s-k-n that names immobility — one who no longer has the strength to move.

versets.trad: So woe to those who pray,
versets.commentaire: The verse is startling: “woe to those who pray”. The word wayl (وَيْل) — “woe!” — is among the strongest interjections of Quranic speech. But what follows narrows it: not woe to all who pray, but to those whose prayer the next verses will describe — the heedless, the showy. The sūra aims at what the Quran will name elsewhere as “the prayer of the hypocrites”: a gesture without heart.

mots.def: linking particle: so, then.
wayl: interjection of threat or lament: woe!; among the strongest Quranic expressions.
muṣallī: one who prays; active participle of ṣallā. Preposition li- + plural definite noun.
mots.gram: preposition + plural active participle, definite genitive

versets.commentaire: sāhūn (سَاهُون) — heedless, negligent, forgetful. Commentators distinguish several senses: those who delay prayer past its time, those who fail to complete bowings and prostrations, those who pray with the heart elsewhere. A ḥadīth describes such prayer: “This is the prayer of the hypocrite: he watches the sun until it is between the two horns of Satan, then he rises and pecks out four cycles in which he remembers God but little.” The sūra does not condemn prayer itself — it names the distance between the gesture and the presence.

mots.def: masc. plur. relative pronoun: those who.
masc. plur. personal pronoun: they; in isolation, reinforces the subject.
preposition: from, regarding; here introduces what they are heedless of.
ṣalāt: the ritual prayer; with the plural suffix pronoun: their prayer.
sāhī: heedless, forgetful; active participle of sahā. Plural: sāhūn.

versets.commentaire: yurāʾūn (يُرَاءُون): they do things to be seen. This is the word riyāʾ (رِيَاء) — ostentation —, a central fault in Muslim spirituality: to act for the gaze of men rather than the Face of God. The Quran says elsewhere of the hypocrites: “When they pray, they pray lazily, to be seen of men” (4:142). The sūra here touches the finest inversion: one may perform the gesture of devotion perfectly, and have lost devotion itself.

mots.def: masc. plur. relative pronoun.
masc. plur. personal pronoun.
rāʾā: the derived form of raʾā (to see); “to make oneself seen”, “to act to be seen” — ostentation, riyāʾ.

versets.commentaire: al-māʿūn (المَاعُون): the word that gives the sūra its name. Commentators give it a broad meaning: what is shared between neighbours — the pickax, the pot, the bucket —, the utensils one lends, but also the alms that are due (the zakāt), or any small service by which common life is held together. To refuse the māʿūn is to break the very ordinary fabric of human solidarity. The sūra closes there, on this tiny detail: religion is tested not only in great things, but in the small — an object lent, a face listened to, a daily kindness given.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
manaʿa: to refuse, to withhold, to keep from access; imperfect plural.

mots.sens: the small kindnesses, the daily aid
mots.def: māʿūn: what is of use (shared utensils, alms due, small services between neighbours); that without which common life cannot stand.
